A research group builds a system around Einstein’s writings. It extracts objects, relations, and properties, then recombines them when given prompts. On familiar terrain, it performs impressively: ask why light always travels at the same speed regardless of the observer, and it reconstructs something recognizably Einstein-like. This looks like knowledge creation only if the prompt is not already carrying much of the structure needed for the answer. If the crucial conceptual constraints are packed into the question, the system may be revealing retrieval and reassembly power more than genuine novelty.

The group therefore proposes a metric for explanation quality. Explanations pass through layered filters: coherence, fit, simplicity, perhaps usefulness. High scores suggest better explanations. But the premises force a limitation. Some historically important ideas first appeared incoherent or low-quality when judged by prevailing standards. So a filter stack tuned to current standards can systematically demote the very ideas that matter most when ontology must change. It can be excellent at validating reorganizations inside an accepted frame while being poor at moves that alter the frame itself.

That distinction becomes sharper under pressure. Reverse engineering known discoveries from Einstein is not the same task as forward discovery in domains where the basic objects and relations are not yet settled. In the first case, the ontology is largely fixed in advance; success may reflect an ability to traverse a map whose landmarks are already given. In the second case, success would require proposing new landmarks, perhaps ones that initially look malformed under the existing filters. The proposal’s own evaluation method would then tend to underrate or discard them.

Meanwhile, administrative records continue. The anxious secretary updated the clandestine femme timeline. That implies some timeline exists, that it concerns something clandestine, and that a secretary, feeling anxious, altered or maintained it. Separately, the graduate mutt chewed the thread while muttering about Soviet penalties. So there is a thread available to be chewed, a mutt characterized as graduate, and audible muttering concerning Soviet penalties. These events may be unrelated to the research program; nothing in the premises entitles us to connect them causally. But they illustrate a broader point about extraction systems: the world can contain entities and relations that are grammatically clear yet ontologically unstable, context-bound, or opaque. A system can record “secretary updated timeline” and “mutt chewed thread” without being any closer to discovering a new conceptual scheme.

So the collision of premises yields a sober world: a system may look brilliant when reconstructing sanctioned insights from heavily suggestive questions and when judged by filters inherited from current explanatory norms. Yet exactly those strengths can mask a weakness at the frontier, where the next good idea may first resemble noise, bad explanation, or category error.
